summary: McKenzie argues that apologizing to customers is one of the most powerful and cost-free tools in customer service. He builds on advice from TryBeta.com about taking blame for all mistakes, expanding it into a broader philosophy: sincere apologies combined with immediate action plans create lasting positive impressions. He advocates minimizing ego involvement, offering refunds proactively (which few customers actually take), and using small scripted apologies to smooth over minor friction. He draws on his experience at both his software business and his day job, plus observations about Japanese customer service culture.
key_points: Sincere apologies are free and create disproportionately positive customer impressions that last for the entire business relationship
Offering refunds proactively is a power move — very few customers take you up on it, but they remember the offer indefinitely
Ego is the main barrier to effective apologizing; separating your identity from your product makes apologies natural
Small scripted apologies for minor inconveniences are a form of excellence — getting the little things right compounds
Blaming inanimate objects for customer mistakes is the customer service equivalent of a white lie and is perfectly acceptable
money_quotes: Apologizing literally works magic on people, particularly sincere apologizing (not 'I'm sorry you're too stupid to use our software but thats the way it is').
Very few people will actually take you up on that offer, but they will remember you making it for the duration they do business with you.
Its a little thing, but excellence is getting the little things right.
My software is my baby, but its not so much a part of me that I can't count to five when someone says they can't use it.
'The computer is acting up' is the 'No, honey, that dress looks great on you' of customer service.
Inanimate objects which you own rarely resent being blamed for your customers' mistakes.
And it costs absolutely nothing to do.
key_insight: A sincere apology costs nothing but creates lasting goodwill, and proactively offering to make things right builds more loyalty than never making mistakes would.
